entry.title: Re: Lock Some fields of PR through ABAP Code
entry.content:
I reformulate

(ME_PROCESS_REQ_CUST)

Basically the "customer allowed" BAdI only handle "customer fields". But you could use the process_header methid to remove user changes of the field (and raise a waning message)

(ME_PROCESS_REQ)

To actually "grey" some standard fields, you must use the "SAP only allowed" BAdI, but as you are not allowed to create a new implementation, select a standard active implementation to divert and enhance (e.g. implicit enhancement points at start and end of methods) /Or/ find/use such enhancement in the main program (e.g. set a break-point at start of the FIELDSELECTION_HEADER method of an active implementing class and debug to find where it's called in standard)

entry.title: Exploit standard functionalties to manage Pipeline processes in SAP
entry.content:
1. Introduction

Pipeline is the process of consuming materials in the production process directly from a pipeline (e.g.: oil), from a pipe (e.g.: mains water), or via a cable (e.g.: electricity). This business process has the following main characteristics in SAP:

  • There are no purchasing documents or agreements created for Pipeline materials other than the Purchasing Info-record which is maintained with the negotiated conditions/prices and tax code
  • There is no Good Receipt posting to receive or invoice for pipeline materials consumptions
  • The vendor provide the pipeline materials at your plant/premises, and whenever you need it, you consume it  via goods issue to cost center or production order
  • There will be a meter which will track how much you have consumed
  • The vendor is not invoicing your Company but it receives the consumption details before payment posting
  • Usually, the vendor is paid periodically, settling the recorded consumptions for the liabilities
  • Often, cleaning robots are used to perform pipeline maintenance ad inspect tasks. In fact, these autonomous robots can navigate both the interior and exterior of pipelines, performing essential tasks like inspection, maintenance, and hazard detection. Whether remotely controlled by personnel or managed automatically by computer systems, they bring a new level of efficiency and safety to pipeline operations. From ensuring smooth flow to detecting potential issues, these robots are the future of pipeline management.

The goal of this process is to allow larger quantities of materials to be continuously consumed in the production process without holding them in the stock. The pipeline process can only be successful where the usage of materials is repetitive and relatively consistent through a time frame. This results in reduced costs and a streamlined supply chain process.  In case the vendor is not able to respect the deliveries, he is penalized and the end result could be the cancellation of the pipeline process or higher prices to cover the business disruptions.

2. Pipeline Process in SAP – Overview

The main business activities to be performed in the SAP are reported in the Figure-1:

Figure 1 – Main activities involved in the Vendor’s Pipeline process

3. System Activities

The business activities that will be shown in the system are the following:

  • Master Data Management
    • Create Material Master
    • Create Vendor Master
    • Create Purchasing Info Record
    • Maintain Condition Record
    • Create Cost Center Group
    • Create Cost Center Standard Hierarchy
    • Create Cost Center
    • Create G/L Accounts
  • Transactional Data
    • Create the liability/ Post the Goods Issue
    • Pipeline Settlement (Display and Settle the Consumption to Post Invoice)
    • Handle vendor’s communications
    • Display the financial document associated with the invoice
    • Register the payment
  • Reports
    • Logistics (list of material documents created for consumptions)
    • Finance (list of accounting documents posted for Vendor’s Account)
  • IMG settings
    • Logistics (material type and standard purchasing organization)
    • Finance (Automatic Account Determination settings)

3.1 Master Data Management

3.1.1 Material Master Data

The master data management team will create pipeline materials via transaction MM01 or following the menu path: Logistics > Materials Management > Material Master > Material > Create (General) > Immediately. This kind of materials are defined “pipeline” materials and are represented in SAP by the material type PIPE. Then, define the mandatory data for the Basis data 1 (Base Unit of Measure and material Group). In the purchasing view, specify the buyer or group of buyers resposnible for such material. In the accounting 1 view, specify the valuation class, price control, price unit, initial moving average price and standard price. Save the data entered, the system will give the material number “Material 12 created”.

3.1.2 Create Vendor

The master data management team will create the vendor via transaction BP or following the menu path: Logistics > Settlement Management > Environment > Master Data > Maintain Business Partner. Specify as BP Role “00000” and enter the address of your BP. 

Save the data entered, the system will give the BP number “Business partner 111 created”. 

Create the Purchasing view, choosing as BP role “FLVN01”. 

Enter the Purchasing details (e.g.: Purchasing Data and Partner Functions). 

Saving the data, the system will give the confirmation message “Changes have been saved”.

Create the Company Code “Z001” view, choosing as BP role “FLVN00”.

Enter the Company Code details (e.g.: Reconciliation Account and sort key).

Saving the data, the system will give the confirmation message “Changes have been saved”.

3.1.3 Create Purchasing Info Record

The purchasing department will  create the pipeline purchasing info record using the transaction ME11 or follow the menu path: Logistics > Materials Management > Purchasing > Master Data > Info Record > Create.

  • In the selection screen, enter the supplier, material, standard purchasing organization, plan and info category “Pipeline”.
  • Note that if the Plant is having more than one purchase organization then create info record with that plant standard purchase organization only and standard purchase organization info record price only will be relevant for automatic settlement.
  • In the “General Data” screen, specify the supplier and Origin data as needed.
  • In the “Purchasing Organization data 1” screen, specify the control and conditions details.
  • Note that, apart from the price details, you need to also enter the tax code otherwise you will get an error during the settlement posting.
  • That’s why some Organization prefer to also have the tax code filed mandatory in the Purchasing Info Record.
  • Then, saving the data, the system will generate the Purchasing info record document number “Purchasing info record 5300000013 PO01 P D001 created”.

3.1.4 Maintain Condition Record

The condition records are prerequisites to generate the expected output to send pipeline settlement document to vendor or else document number will not create during pipeline settlement.
The condition record can be maintained using the transaction code MRM1. In the selection screen, enter the output type “KONS” and select the key combination. After giving the vendor number click on “communication” and enter required details, like output device, number of message, spool request name, save it. After specifying the output details, upon saving the system will give the information message “Condition records saved”.

3.1.5 Create Cost Center Group

The controlling department will create the cost center groups either using the transaction KSH1 or following the manu path: Accounting > Controlling > Cost Center Accounting > Master Data > Cost Center Group> Create. In our case, the Standard Cost center Hierarchy “STD1” contains our Cost Center “CC2 - Utilities”.

3.1.6 Create Standard Costs Center Hierarchy

After creating a Cost Center Group, you can assign it to the Cost Center Standard Hierarchy, which is a hierarchical structure to depict your entire Costing Area. You will group your cost centers and you will assign these groups to your standard hierarchy, each group and subgroup will represent different levels of Nodes in Hierarchy, either using the transaction OKEON or following the menu path: Accounting > Controlling > Cost Center Accounting > Master Data > Standard Hierarchy > Change. Now, whenever you create your cost center master data, you must give the "Hierarchy Area", where in you have to give any of the Cost Center Group which has been assigned to Cost Center Hierarchy.  In case you feel that the cost center which you are going to create does not fall under any cost center group, then you can directly take the Original Standard Hierarchy name (Highest Node). Note that in this transaction, you can also create a new cost center.

3.1.7 Create Cost Center

The controlling department will create the cost center either using the transaction KS01 or following the menu path: Accounting > Controlling > Cost Center Accounting > Master Data > Cost Center > Individual Processing > Create. In  our case, we will use the Cost Center “CC2 – Utilities” linked to the Company Code “Z001” with validity from 01.01.2024 to 31.12.9999.

Basic Data Tab

  • User Responsible
  • Person Responsible
  • Department
  • Cost center Category “F - Production”
  • Hierarchy Area “STD1”
  • Business Area “BA01”
  • Currency “EUR”

Control Tab

  • Record qty box flagged
  • Commitment update box flagged

3.1.8 Create G/L Accounts

The finance department will create the G/L account either via transaction FS00 or following the menu path: Accounting > Financial Accounting > General Ledger > Master Records > G/L Accounts > Individual Processing > Centrally. In  our case, we will use the following G/L accounts, as listed in the Table-1.

G/L Account Number

Description

Type

Control Data Tab

Create/bank/interest Tab

400000

Consumption, raw material 1

· G/L Account Type: P - Primary Costs or Revenue

· Account Group: MAT – Materials Management Accounts

· P&L Statement Account Type: X

 

 

· Account Currency: EUR

· Tax Category: “-“ Only input tax allowed

· Sort key: 008 - Cost center

· CElem category: 1 – Primary costs/cost-reducing revenues

· Field status group: G003 “Material consumption accounts”

· Post Automatic Only: Option flagged

 

 

 

 

160000

Accounts payable- domestic

· G/L Account Type: X – Balance Sheet Account

· Account Group: GL – general GL Accounts

· P&L Statement Account Type: ‘ ‘

· Account Currency: EUR

· Tax Category: “-“ Only input tax allowed

· Reconciliation Account for Acct Type: K - Vendors

· Field status group: G067 “Reconciliation accounts”

· Post Automatic Only: Option flagged

 

 

169900

Payments from consignment warehouse

· G/L Account Type: X – Balance Sheet Account

· Account Group: GL – general GL Accounts

· P&L Statement Account Type: ‘ ‘

 

 

 

 

· Account Currency: EUR

· Tax Category: “-“ Only input tax allowed

· Open Item Management: Option flagged

· Sort key: 029 – Posting, month, vendor

· Reconciliation Account for Acct Type: K - Vendors

· Field status group: G059 “Inventory accounting material stock acct”

· Post Automatically Only: Option flagged

 

 

 

 

 

 

154000

 

Input Tax

· G/L Account Type: X – Balance Sheet Account

· Account Group: GL – general GL Accounts

· P&L Statement Account Type: ‘ ‘

 

 

· Account Currency: EUR

· Balances in Local Currency Only: Option flagged

· Tax Category: < Input Tax Account

· Open Item Management: Option flagged

· Field status group: G014 “MM adjustment accounts”

· Post Automatically Only: Option flagged

 

 

 

 

Table 1 – Relevant G/L Accounts

3.2 Transactional Data Management

3.2.1 Create Vendor Liability

Pipeline withdrawals, owned by production execution/operation team, differ from other goods movements in that they are entered with the special stock indicator “P”.

  • The withdrawal is valuated at the price defined in the pipeline info record
  • The pipeline withdrawal has no effect on existing warehouse stock or on the availability of the material. 
  • The pipeline withdrawal results in a vendor liability that must be settled periodically in a similar way to the management of consignment stocks. In the standard system the same account is used for both pipeline and consignment liabilities, but a different pipeline liabilities account can be defined in Customizing
  • A consumption history is kept
  • You can enter a pipeline withdrawal for an order or for a cost center.

To post the liability, the operation team can either use the transaction MIGO or follow the menu path: Logistics > Materials Management > Inventory Management > Goods Movement > Goods Movement. Then, specify the data as reported in the following Figure-2.

Figure 2 – Create Goods Issue (Data Entry)

After specifying the required data and upon saving, the system will give the material document number, as shown in the Figure-3.

Figure 3 – Create Goods Issue (Document Number)

3.2.2 Display Vendor Liability

To post the liability, the operation team can either use the transaction MIGO or follow the menu path: Logistics > Materials Management > Inventory Management > Goods Movement > Goods Movement. Then, specify the data as reported in the Figure-4.

Figure 4 – Display Material Document

Then, pressing the option “FI Documents” and selecting the accounting documents, the system will show the FI/CO documents, as reported in the Figure-5 and Figure-6.

Figure 5 – Display Accounting Document

As you can see from the above screen, the liability has been credited “H-Credit” and the consumption account has been debited “S-Debit”.

Figure 6 – Display Controlling Document

3.2.3 Pipeline Settlement

  • Usually the purchasing department will run the transaction in display mode and then share to the vendors the details, then Account Payable will run the transaction in posting mode to settle the consumptions.
  • If the business shows that there is no need of alignment with the vendor regarding the consumptions done, then the settlement process would likely be regularly scheduled via batch and controlled by A/P.
  • The pipeline postings can be displayed and settled via a single transaction “MRKO” or following the menu path: Information Systems > General Report Selection > Materials Management > Inventory Management > Settle Consignment/Pipeline Liabilities

In the Figure-7 and Figure-8, you can see respectively the selection and output screen of the settlement program to display the consumption of the pipeline materials.

Figure 7 – Pipeline Settlement (Selection Screen – Display mode)

Executing the program, you can see the details of the pipeline materials that have been consumed and need to the settle for vendor’s payment.

Figure 8 – Pipeline Settlement (Output Screen – Display mode)

In the Figure-9 and Figure-10, you can see respectively the selection and output screen of the settlement program to settle the consumption of the pipeline materials.

Figure 9 – Pipeline Settlement (Selection Screen – Settle mode)

Executing the program, you can see the details of the pipeline materials that have been consumed and need to the settle for vendor’s payment along with the invoice automatically generated.

Figure 10 – Pipeline Settlement (Output Screen – Settle mode)

3.2.4 Display Invoice Document

You can display the invoice created in the previous step via transaction FB03 or following the menu path: Accounting > Financial Accounting > General Ledger > Document > Display. Then, enter the document number, company code and FY, as shown in the Figure-11.

Figure 11 – Invoice Document (Selection Screen)

Then, pressing “enter”, the system will show the accounting entries for pipeline settlement, as shown in the Figure-12.

Figure 12 – Invoice Document (data Entry View)

3.2.5 Invoice Message Processing

Printing/transmitting the settlement document to vendor with output type “KONS”  for the invoice created in MRKO, is done via transaction MR91. In the Figure-13, you can find the selection screen of such program.

Figure 13 – Message from Invoice Verification (Selection Screen)

Then, pressing “Execute”, the system will show the document to be processed, as shown in the Figure-14.

Figure 14 – Message from Invoice Verification (Output Screen)

Then, selecting the document to be processed and pressing “Process”, the system will give the information message as shown in the Figure-15.

Figure 15 – Message from Invoice Verification (Confirmation Message)

Important Note

If you will get an ABAP Runtime Error upon the printing preview, via MR91 or SP01, due to this issue: Exception condition "FEATURE_NOT_SUPPORTED" triggered, then you can refer to the OSS Note# https://me.sap.com/notes/0003376098.

3.2.6 Payment Run

  • The payment can be done either via regular process or via credit note (if legally admitted)
  • This process is planned by the account payable department and executed by treasury
  • The standard transactions to perform  the payments are the followig
    • F110 “Parameters for Automatic Payment” also reached via menu path: Accounting > Financial Accounting > Accounts Receivable > Periodic Processing > Payments. This transaction automates the payment process by selecting vendor invoices due for payment, generating payment files and post the payment documents on the system, widely used for regular payments run.
    • F-53 “Post Outgoing Payments”, manually triggered, also reached via menu path: Accounting > Financial Accounting > Accounts Payable > Document Entry > Outgoing Payment > Post. This transaction is used to post outgoing payments, such as vendor payments. It updates the accounts payable and general ledger accordingly.
    • F-58 “Payment with Printout”, manually triggered, also reached via manu path: Accounting > Financial Accounting > Accounts Payable > Document Entry > Outgoing Payment > Post + Print Forms. This transaction is used to execute payment run, which generate payment documents for multiple vendor invoices based on defined selection criteria.

Important Note

The pipeline process is strongly relay on the availability of the material and it assumes that the availability is always granted by the selected vendors. In case of unexpected issues (e.g.: the vendor could not supply pipeline material continuously), the production department will be heavily impacted along with the customer’s deliveries. In such cases, it is recommended to create a service order with your vendors to manage the bonus / penalty (in the line item detail, delivery tab, you need to unflag the option “Goods Receipt”). Then, the Accounts Payable department can book subsequent debit / credit via transaction code “MIRO”, considering as reference the previous service order created.                                                                                       

3.3 Reports

In the following paragraph you can find the main standard reports available in the system to monitor this flow from logistics and financial points of view.

In the Figure-16 and Figure-17, you can find the selection and output screens of the “Material Document List” report, reached via transaction MB51.

Figure 16 – Material Document List (Selection Screen)

 

Figure 17 – Material Document List (Output Screen)

In the Figure-18 and Figure-19, you can find the selection and output screens of the “Vendor Line Item Display” report, reached via transaction FBL1N.

This transaction is used to display the line-items details for a specific vendor, like information on invoices, payments, and open items with associated due dates to be processed.

Figure 18 – Vendor Line Item Display (Selection Screen)

 

Figure 19 – Vendor Line Item Display (Output Screen)

3.4 Customizing Settings

In the following paragraphs I am going to report only the most important customizing settings to be checked in the MM and FI modules.

3.4.1 Materials Management relevant customizing

a. Verify the Material type used for Pipeline

To check the attribute of the material type “PIPE”, you can use the IMG transaction OMS2 and

Select material type PIPE and note the following attributes:

  • In the special material type section, the “pipeline mandatory flag” is on
  • In the folder Quality/value updating, the Qty updating and Value updating are not flagged for your Valuation Areas (which are your Plants)

b. Set up Standard Purchasing Organization

In case your Plant has more than one purchasing organization, one of the purchasing organizations must be designated as standard Purchase Organization to be used for the transaction of pipeline material, consignment material or stock transfer orders. You can set-up the default values, following IMG menu path: SPRO > Enterprise Structure > Materials Management > Assign standard purchasing organization to plant. In our case, the Plant “D001” has been linked with the Standard Purchasing Organization “PO01”

3.4.2 Financial Accounting Settings

c. Settings for the Automatic Account Determination via IMG transaction OBYC

The following accounts are updated during GI (MvT 201-P):

  • Dr Consumption via GBB-VBR Offset inventory account (consumption)
  • Cr Vendor payable via KON-PIP(-) Amount payable (consignment)

The following accounts are updated during the settlement (MRKO):

  • Dr Vendor payable via KON-PIP(+) Amount payable (consignment)
  • Cr Vendor payable via Vendor reconciliation account in vendor master

4. Conclusion

Following this article, the readers will be able to check the main IMG settings in system, master data and record the transactional data needed to manage the pipeline processes. Additionally, this can be used as guideline, across project phases, for anybody involved in SAP Logistic activities, despite the release of the system.

entry.title: SOLUTION MANAGER TEST SUITE - Step-by-Step Guide for Test Manager Workflow in SAP Solution Manager
entry.content:
1. Test Case Creation

Step 1: Open the Test Steps Designer Application

  • Navigate to:
    SPRO -> SAP Solution Manager -> Focused Build -> Test Suite Extensions -> Test Steps -> Folders for Test Steps Application
  • Use the following URL to access the Test Steps Designer:
     
    https://solman.[customer_name].com/sap/bc/ui5_ui5/ui2/ushell/shells/abap/FioriLaunchpad.html?sap-client=001&sap-language=EN

Step 2: Create a New Test Case

  • In the Test Steps Designer, click on "Create".
  • Provide the Test Case Name and select a folder where the case will be stored.
  • Add basic details for your test case (e.g., steps, description, instructions, and expected results for each step).
  • You can also add a team or additional test steps if necessary.

Step 3: Upload Test Steps via CSV (Option 2)

  • Download a sample CSV template from the Test Steps Designer.
  • Fill in the test data in the template.
  • Save the file in .csv format and then upload it via the Test Steps Designer.

2. Adding Test Result Attributes

Step 4: Configure Test Result Attributes

  • Navigate to:
    SPRO -> SAP Solution Manager -> Focused Build -> Test Suite Extensions -> Test Steps -> Customer Fields for Test Steps Result Attributes
  • You can add Test Result Attributes below each test step. Select the appropriate attribute for each step or case.

3. Assigning Test Case to Solution Documentation

Step 5: Manually Assign Test Case to Solution Documentation (Option 1)

  • Navigate to the solution documentation.
  • Search for the Orphan Test Case, and click OK to assign it.
  • The test case will now be available in the scenario.

Step 6: Automatic Assignment (Option 2)

  • Click on an existing test case in the folder where you want to assign other test cases automatically.
  • Download the existing test case and extract the SolDoc Path Occ ID.
  • Use this ID in the SolDoc Path Occ ID cell in the Excel template for future test case uploads to automate the process.

4. Test Plan and Sequence Creation

Step 7: Create Test Plans and Sequences

  • After test case creation, proceed to create the Test Plan and Test Sequence for structured execution of tests.

5. Tester Assignment

Step 8: Assign Testers

  • Assign testers to the test cases and packages created, ensuring proper execution responsibility is designated.

This structure provides a clear and easy-to-follow process for managing test cases in SAP Solution Manager. Please ask if you have any questions/calrifications in the Reply.

entry.title: Enabling WEBGUI Firefighter in SAP GRC
entry.content:
Purpose

SAP has enabled the feature to execute Fire Fighter for web-based transactions using WEB-GUI Firefighter functionality. Now FF users can execute NWBC, FIORI and other web-based transactions using WEB-GUI FF. Web GUI is enabled for the cases where some web apps are not accessible via SAP GUI. In this blog, I am going to provide the step-by-step process for enabling WEBGUI firefighter, pre-requisites, limitations and usage.

 

Configuration

 This configuration is applicable for centralized Firefighter configurations in SAP GRC 12.0, SP17

 

Activation steps

Actions needs to be performed in GRC system

GRC Team

  • Go to SPRO à IMG à Governance, Risk and Compliance à Access Control à Maintain Connector Settings

  • Select the connector à Assign Attributes to the connector
  • For Embedded Fiori systems and Fiori systems we have to add SESSION_MANAGER and /UI2/FLP parameter

  • For the systems, where Fiori is not there, we just have to add SESSION_MANAGER

Basis Team

  • Offline coActivate below SICF services:

         /default_host/sap/bc/gui/sap/its/webgui

        /default_host/sap/public/myssocntl

        /default_host/sap/public/bc/ur

       /default_host/sap/public/bc/its/mimes

  • Below RZ10 parameters needs to be maintained with mentioned parameter values.

               Login/accept_sso2_ticket = 1

               login/create_sso2_ticket = 2

Actions needs to be performed in PLUG-IN system

Basis Team

  • Activate below SICF services

              /default_host/sap/bc/gui/sap/its/webgui

              /default_host/sap/public/myssocntl

  • Below RZ10 parameters needs to be maintained with mentioned parameter values

         login/accept_sso2_ticket = 1

         login/create_sso2_ticket = 2

  • ~NO_LOGON_USEREXIT = 1 parameter needs to be maintained under GUI configuration in WEBGUI SICF service under path “/default_host/sap/bc/gui/sap/its/”

Click on GUI configuration and maintain the ~NO_LOGON_USEREXIT=1

  • Whitelisting of WEBGUI URL’s is required – where basis team confirmed on the process.
    • Go to Uconcockpit transaction.
    • Cross verify, if paths are whitelisted or not. If not kindly whitelist the required path
    • Add entry - https *.azure.intra.xxxxxx, port = *  /sap/bc/gui/sap/its/* and whitelist if any path is blocked with below parameters.  *.azure.intra.xxxx  port = *
    • Activate and ask GRC team to validate in all systems.

Below is the reference Screenshot.

Fiori Team

Create a Fiori Tile “GRC_FireFighter WebGUI” for end user and create a portal role to access the WEBGUI FF functionality by clicking on that Tile.

 

Pre-requisite to access FFID via Web GUI

  • FFID for required plug-in system should be assigned to FF users.
  • To access any backend systems WEBGUI FF ID, user should login to Fiori portal link to access GRC-Firefighter- WEBGUI tile.

Limitations

  1. Web-Gui Browser needs to be closed and should be refreshed during logout.
  2. SAP has limited the FF login using multiple FF IDs for same FF user and system. (Reference SAP note: 2672041 - GRC 12.0: Web Based Emergency Access Management)
  3. Not all FF logs will be captured related to web applications, especially for Web Dynpro tiles (Reference SAP note: 2952305 about FF log limitation)

Functionality availability

If you have different backend system then below table provides information about what the functionality available in the corresponding system on like WEBGUI FF logon, WEGGUI TCODES, GUI Tiles, WDA tiles, UI5 tile, BRF+, SOAMANAGER, NWBC, CRM_UI etc. and this may change going forward.

System

WEBGUI FF logon- GRC

WEBGUI TCODES

GUI tile

WDA tile

UI5 tile

BRF+

SOAMANAGER

NWBC

CRM_UI

S4

Available

Available

Summary

In this blog, I explored the significance of enabling SAP WEBGUI Firefighter features, configuration, pre-requisite, limitation, functionality available.

In upcoming blog, I will provide fixes of all issues that encountered during the WEBGUI FF functionality testing.

entry.title: SAP Trading Partner Management - Part II - Data
entry.content:
As mentioned in the previous post - TPM overview, Data is handled by SAP Integration Advisor and involves Message Implementation Guidelines (MIG) and Mapping Guidelines (MAG).

Let's prepare a very simple scenario, where we will be receiving a Purchase order in IDoc format, and map it 1:1 with a tiny change in the data.

MIG

First we create a MIG for the Purchase order. In the Design menu, we choose MIGs and click Create.

If we are fine with standard IDocs, we keep 'Standard' highlighted and select the type of source system - in our case SAP S/4HANA on-premise IDoc.

Then we get plenty of standard IDoc types to choose from. We select ORDERS.ORDES05 and on the following screen the version. The default values are limited, but it can be overwritten later. We select 1809_FPS02.

On the next screen we can upload our sample IDoc file if we have one, or we can just skip this step.

Finally, this is our summary and we can proceed with creating our MIG. As 'Own Business Context' is a mandatory section, we select a Business Process which best describes our message exchange. In this case - Manage Purchase Order.

After we save our MIG, we activate it in the top right menu.

MAG

In the left Design menu, we choose MAGs and click Create. As source MIG, we select our previously created MIG.

As target MIG, we can actually use the same MIG, as we will be just mapping 1:1. This is a simplified scenario to show the principles, we are not going to deep dive into mapping here. Nevertheless, some basic mapping is necessary, so let's do it.

Note, that we don't name the MAG in our own matter. The name of the MAG is simply 'source MIG to target MIG'.

After we save our MAG, the mapping screen appears automatically and we easily drag a line from the parent element of the source MIG to the parent element of the target MIG. This means that we are mapping all fields.

Now let's map something very trivial. We look for the element CREDAT (date of creation) and just modify the format from CCYYMMDD to DDMMCCYY. Again we drag the line from left to right and the mapping is just a selection from a dropdown list.

Then we need to activate the MAG the same way we did the MIG.

Now our work is done and our data artifacts MIG and MAG are ready to be used in TPM configuration.

entry.title: Re: AI in SAP Ariba Invoicing - Intelligent Invoice Conversion
entry.content:
Hello @rahulyadav,

The supplier must provide the ANID (only) of the valid SAP Business Network buyer account in the subject line of the email while sending the invoice for processing using IIC. IIC does not validate the buyer details that is extracted from the invoice with the buyer account to use.

Kindly refer this link for more details - NP-38357: Intelligent Invoice Conversion Support for Inbound Email Channel to Receive PDF and Scanned Invoices.

entry.title: Read \ Write Data between HANA Datalake and HANA Cloud DB
entry.content:
A simple guide to Read \ Write table data between SAP HANA Datalake and SAP HANA Cloud DB. 

Key topics include:

  • Export from HANA Cloud and Import into HANA Datalake Filesystem using CSV and PARQUET formats.
  • Export from HANA Datalake Relational Engine and import to the same or different HANA Datalake Filesystem in CSV and PARQUET formats.

 

Export from HANA Cloud and Import into HANA Datalake Filesystem using CSV and PARQUET formats

Please note that all the SQL queries are executed in the SQL Console of HANA Cloud DB

1. Creating and Verifying the PSE (Personal Security Environment)

Step 1: Create PSE
Use the following SQL query to create a PSE named 'TESTING':

 

CREATE PSE TESTING;

 

Step 2: Cross-verify the newly created PSE
To verify that the PSE has been successfully created, run:

 

SELECT * FROM PSES;

 

2. Download and Add DigiCertGlobalRootCA Certificate

Step 3: Download the DigiCertGlobalRootCA certificate
Download the certificate from this link: https://dl.cacerts.digicert.com/DigiCertGlobalRootCA.crt.pem 

Step 4: Copy Certificate Content
Open the downloaded certificate in a text editor and copy the content between "-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----" and "-----END CERTIFICATE-----".

3. Create Certificate in HANA Cloud DB

Step 5: Create Certificate using SQL
Create the certificate in HANA Cloud DB using the following SQL query:

 

CREATE CERTIFICATE TESTING_DIGICERT FROM '
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIDrzCCApegAwIBAgIQCDvgVpBCRrGhdWrJWZHHSjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQUFADBh
...
-----END CERTIFICATE-----';

 

4. Map the DigiCertGlobalRootCA Certificate to the PSE

Step 6: Map the certificate to the PSE
To map the certificate TESTING_DIGICERT to the PSE TESTING, use:

 

ALTER PSE TESTING ADD CERTIFICATE TESTING_DIGICERT;

 

5. Map the HANA Datalake Filesystem Certificate Chain

Step 7: Map the HANA Datalake Filesystem certificate chain
Map the certificate chain using the following query:

 

ALTER PSE TESTING SET OWN CERTIFICATE
'-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
Clientkey
-----END PRIVATE KEY-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MainCertificate
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
Intermediatecertificate
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
RootCertificate
-----END CERTIFICATE-----';

 

Please provide the entire content of the certificate and key between -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE----- & -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY----- and -----END PRIVATE KEY-----

6. Verify the Certificates

Step 8: Verify all certificates
Check all the certificates using:

 

SELECT * FROM CERTIFICATES;

 

Step 9: Verify if certificates are mapped to the PSE
Run the following query to verify certificate mappings:

 

SELECT * FROM PSE_CERTIFICATES;

 

7. Create Credential for Import/Export Operations

Step 10: Create a Credential using the SAPHDLRELOADUNLOAD Component
Create a credential using the following query:

 

CREATE CREDENTIAL FOR COMPONENT 'SAPHANAIMPORTEXPORT' PURPOSE 'TESTPURP' TYPE 'X509' PSE TESTING;

 

Note that PURPOSE is the name of the Credential. The TYPE is X509 as we are dealing with certificates. Map it to newly created PSE.

Step 11: Cross-verify Credential
To verify the credential, run:

 

SELECT * FROM CREDENTIALS;

 

8. Export / Import Operations from / to HANA Datalake Filesystem and HANA Cloud DB

Export Table Data to CSV File

Use the following query to export data from the TESTMYTABLE table which is present in the HANA Cloud DB as a CSV file onto HANA Datalake Filesystem:

 

EXPORT INTO CSV FILE
'hdlfs://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.files.hdl.prod-eu10.hanacloud.ondemand.com/path_to_file/data.csv'
FROM TESTMYTABLE
WITH
CREDENTIAL 'TESTPURP'
COLUMN LIST IN FIRST ROW;

 

Import Data from CSV File to Table

Use the following query to import the CSV file which is present in the HANA Datalake Filesystem into HANA Cloud DB:

 

IMPORT FROM CSV FILE 'hdlfs://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.files.hdl.prod-eu10.hanacloud.ondemand.com/path_to_file/data.csv'
INTO TESTMYTABLE WITH
CREDENTIAL 'TESTPURP'
COLUMN LIST IN FIRST ROW
FAIL ON INVALID DATA;

 

Export Table Data to PARQUET File

Use the following query to export data from the TESTMYTABLE table which is present in the HANA Cloud DB as a PARQUET file onto HANA Datalake Filesystem:

 

EXPORT INTO PARQUET FILE 'hdlfs://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.files.hdl.prod-eu10.hanacloud.ondemand.com/path_to_file/data.parquet'
FROM (SELECT * FROM TESTMYTABLE)
WITH CREDENTIAL 'TESTPURP';

 

Import Data from PARQUET file to Table

Use the following query to import the PARQUET file which is present in the HANA Datalake Filesystem into HANA Cloud DB:

 

IMPORT FROM PARQUET FILE 'hdlfs://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.files.hdl.prod-eu10.hanacloud.ondemand.com/path_to_file/data.parquet'
INTO TESTMYTABLE
WITH CREDENTIAL 'TESTPURP';

 

Please note that table TESTMYTABLE is an example. Replace it with valid table. Also, kindly change the HANA Datalake REST API Endpoint URL as per the requirement.

 

Export from HANA Datalake Relational Engine and import to the same or different HANA Datalake Filesystem in CSV and PARQUET formats

Please note that all the SQL queries are executed in the SQL Console of HANA Datalake Relational Engine.

Export / Import Operations from / to same HANA Datalake Relational Engine and HANA Datalake Filesystem

Export Table Data to PARQUET File

Use the following query to export data from the TESTMYTABLE table which is present in the HANA Datalake Relational Engine as a PARQUET file onto the same HANA Datalake Filesystem:

 

UNLOAD SELECT * FROM TESTMYTABLE
INTO FILE 'hdlfs://path_to_file/DATA.parquet'
FORMAT PARQUET

 

Import Data from PARQUET File to Table

Use the following SQL query to load data a PARQUET file which is present in HANA Datalake Filesystem into the same HANA Datalake Relational Engine:

 

LOAD TABLE TESTMYTABLE (NAME, ADDRESS)
FROM 'hdlfs://path_to_file/DATA.parquet'
FORMAT PARQUET
ESCAPES OFF;

 

Export Table Data to CSV File

Use the following query to export data from the TESTMYTABLE table which is present in the HANA Datalake Relational Engine as a CSV file onto the same HANA Datalake Filesystem:

 

UNLOAD SELECT * FROM TESTMYTABLE
INTO FILE 'hdlfs://path_to_file/DATA.csv';

 

Import Data from CSV File to Table

Use the following SQL query to load data a CSV file which is present in HANA Datalake Filesystem into the same HANA Datalake Relational Engine:

 

LOAD TABLE TESTMYTABLE (NAME, ADDRESS)
FROM 'hdlfs://path_to_file/DATA.csv'
ESCAPES OFF;

 

Please note that table TESTMYTABLE and it's columns NAME and ADDRESS are examples. Replace them with valid table and it's column names.

 

Export / Import Operations from / to different HANA Datalake Relational Engine and HANA Datalake Filesystem

1. Creating and Verifying the PSE (Personal Security Environment)

Step 1: Create New PSE
Create a new PSE (Personal Security Environment) for the HANA Datalake Relational Engine using the following SQL query:

 

CREATE PSE MYNEWPSE;

 

Step 2: Cross-verify the newly created PSE
To cross-verify that the new PSE has been created, run the following query:

 

SELECT * FROM SYSPSE;

 

2. Adding Certificates to the PSE

Step 3: Find the DigiCertGlobalRootCA Certificate
Find the DigiCertGlobalRootCA certificate and take note of the object_id for mapping it to the newly created PSE:

 

SELECT * FROM SYSCERTIFICATE WHERE cert_name = 'DigiCertRootCA';

 

Step 4: Map the Certificate to the PSE
Use the retrieved object_id to map the DigiCertGlobalRootCA certificate to the newly created PSE:

 

ALTER PSE MYNEWPSE ADD CERTIFICATE 6515;

 

Note: 6515 is example. Replace 6515 with the actual object_id.

Step 5: Map the HANA Datalake Filesystem Certificate Chain
Use the following SQL query to map the certificate chain for the HANA Datalake Filesystem to the PSE:

 

ALTER PSE MYNEWPSE SET OWN CERTIFICATE
'-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
Clientkey
-----END PRIVATE KEY-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MainCertificate
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
Intermediatecertificate
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
RootCertificate
-----END CERTIFICATE-----';

 

Please provide the entire content of the certificate and key between -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE----- & -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY----- and -----END PRIVATE KEY-----

3. Verify the Certificates

Step 6: Cross-verify the Certificates
To ensure that the certificates are correctly loaded, run:

 

SELECT * FROM SYSCERTIFICATE;

 

Step 7: Verify if Certificates are Mapped to the Correct PSE
Check if the certificates are correctly mapped to the newly created PSE:

 

SELECT * FROM SYSPSECERTIFICATE;

 

4. Create Credential for Import/Export Operations

Step 8: Create a Credential
Create a credential that will be used to authenticate export and import operations using the SAPHDLRELOADUNLOAD component:

 

CREATE CREDENTIAL FOR COMPONENT 'SAPHDLRELOADUNLOAD' PURPOSE 'MYNEWTEST' TYPE 'X509' PSE MYNEWPSE;

 

Note that PURPOSE is the name of the Credential. The TYPE is X509 as we are dealing with certificates. Map it to newly created PSE.

Step 9: Verify the Credential
Verify that the credential has been successfully created:

 

SELECT * FROM SYSCREDENTIAL;

 

5. Export / Import Operations from / to different HANA Datalake Relational Engine and HANA Datalake Filesystem

Export Table Data to PARQUET File

Use the following query to export data from the TESTMYTABLE table which is present in the HANA Datalake Relational Engine as a PARQUET file onto the different HANA Datalake Filesystem:

 

UNLOAD SELECT * FROM TESTMYTABLE INTO FILE 'hdlfs://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX/path_to_file/data.parquet'
FORMAT PARQUET
CONNECTION_STRING 'ENDPOINT=https://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.files.hdl.prod-eu10.hanacloud.ondemand.com'
WITH CREDENTIAL 'MYNEWTEST'

 

Import Data from PARQUET File to Table

Use the following SQL query to load data a PARQUET file which is present in different HANA Datalake Filesystem into this HANA Datalake Relational Engine:

 

LOAD TABLE TESTMYTABLE (NAME, ADDRESS)
FROM 'hdlfs://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX/path_to_file/data.parquet'
FORMAT PARQUET
CONNECTION_STRING 'ENDPOINT=https://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.files.hdl.prod-eu10.hanacloud.ondemand.com'
WITH CREDENTIAL 'MYNEWTEST'
FORMAT PARQUET
ESCAPES OFF;

 

Export Table Data to CSV File

Use the following query to export data from the TESTMYTABLE table which is present in the HANA Datalake Relational Engine as a CSV file onto the different HANA Datalake Filesystem:

 

UNLOAD SELECT * FROM TESTMYTABLE INTO FILE 'hdlfs://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX/path_to_file/data.CSV'
CONNECTION_STRING 'ENDPOINT=https://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.files.hdl.prod-eu10.hanacloud.ondemand.com'
WITH CREDENTIAL 'MYNEWTEST'

 

Import Data from CSV File to Table

Use the following SQL query to load data a CSV file which is present in different HANA Datalake Filesystem into this HANA Datalake Relational Engine:

 

LOAD TABLE TESTMYTABLE(NAME, ADDRESS) FROM 'hdlfs://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX/data.CSV'
CONNECTION_STRING 'ENDPOINT=https://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.files.hdl.prod-eu10.hanacloud.ondemand.com'
WITH CREDENTIAL 'MYNEWTEST'
ESCAPES OFF;

 

Please note that table TESTMYTABLE and it's columns NAME and ADDRESS are examples. Replace them with valid table and it's column names. Also, kindly change the HANA Datalake REST API Endpoint URL as per the requirement.

entry.title: Fixed bin replenishment - How to prevent using source bin as same bin that needs to be replenished
entry.content:
As the title suggests, I've successfully implemented fixed bin replenishment to our system. And once any of these fixed bins fall under their minimum bin qty, they are flagged for replenishment, as we hoped they would.

The problem comes during the next step: We noticed that SAP first goes to the very bin that needs to be replenished, and picks that same bin to be the first source bin it will pull material from. And only after depleting that bin, does it move to another source bin (assuming that the replenishment qty is a greater than the available qty of course). We want SAP to block the bin that's being replenished from being used as a source bin for replenishing said bin. I'll explain this better with this example below, I hope.

Lets say the fixed bin is called A00-01. Lets say that the actual quantity of stock in this bin currently is 10, and that the minimum bin qty is set at 20 (meaning the actual quantity fell below the minimum qty triggering it to be replenished, which is what we want). And lets say that the replenishment quantity is 40.

Initially, what we saw happing were two transactions happening in this order:
1) 10 units from A00-01 move right back to A00-01 (leaving qty unchanged at 10 still)
2) 30 (remaining) units move from another source bin to A00-01 (leaving final qty at 40)

We basically want to prevent the first step from happening AT ALL, and go directly to step two except all 40 units are moved from source bin to A00-01, leaving the final quantity after replenishment = 50 units ( aka 10 (initial qty before replenishment) + 40 (replenishment quantity)

We tried changing the search sequence so that other storage types would be picked first, which did prevent this specific scenario from happening, BUT we also realized that we actually WANT it to look at other bins within the same storage type FIRST before it checked other storage types. So this won't work for us.

Hoping someone call tell us if they know how to temporarily block the bin that's being replenished from becoming the source bin while running replenishment. Any help is greatly appreciated!

entry.title: Re: New 2H 2024 SAP Successfactors Time (Tracking) Features
entry.content:
@PetraRana yes, using the enhanced time sheet submit / approve tracking fields will fall under Time Tracking, even if it is only used in reporting.

Best regards

Volker

entry.title: Re: Bring your own Git for SAP BTP ABAP Environment
entry.content:
I wonder...
It seems like there is a discrepancy between the readability and the information in the LIMU objects for the stable transports. Would it not be enough to say that this issue can be solved by putting the source code as a whole in one file, while the different includes are in separate files? One file serves readability, and the others meet the transport demands? 
Or would there be already a kind of transformation logic available that changes the structure from one to the other, which we could use in a pipline while transferring from one git repo to another?

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to schedule a background which runs only from Monday to Friday and 7am to 7pm?
entry.content:
If your job only has one program with one variant, them you are good - just set up the job using BTCAUX10. 

Maintain the jobname, program name and variant name and the time restrictions in it.

 

If your job consists of multiple steps with different programs and program names - then you are really out of luck. Job scheduling options are quite poor with SAP and hence the dependency on external tools.

entry.title: Re: Loipro creation user-exit
entry.content: @andrea_peroni yes, you must create a customer project referencing 'LOI00001' in transaction CMOD and activate any relevant components.
